pairs.question: What was the primary purpose of establishing the Civil Affairs Police Force (CAPF) in British Malaya?
pairs.answer: The primary purpose of establishing the Civil Affairs Police Force (CAPF) was to maintain peace and security in British Malaya after the surrender of the Japanese Imperial Army in August 1945.

pairs.question: Who led the Civil Affairs Police Force (CAPF) when it was first established?
pairs.answer: The Civil Affairs Police Force (CAPF) was led by Commissioner of Police H. B. Longworthy when it was first established in September 1945.

pairs.question: How did the British Military Administration contribute to the establishment of the CAPF?
pairs.answer: The British Military Administration set up the Civil Affairs Police Force (CAPF) as part of its efforts to restore order and security in British Malaya after taking over administration from the Japanese Imperial Army in August 1945.

pairs.question: What was significant about the CAPF in terms of police administration in British Malaya?
pairs.answer: The CAPF was significant because it was the first time a single, centrally administered police force was established for the entire British Malaya, replacing the multiple police forces that had existed prior to the Japanese Occupation (1942–1945).

pairs.question: How many police officers completed the Refresher Courses at the Kuala Lumpur training center by 1946?
pairs.answer: By 1946, a total of 1,512 police officers from all over Malaya had completed the Refresher Courses at the newly built police training center in Kuala Lumpur.

pairs.question: Why did the first recruitment drive of the CAPF in January 1946 reject about 85% of applicants?
pairs.answer: The first recruitment drive of the CAPF rejected about 85% of applicants due to an overwhelming response, as it received far more applications than it could accommodate in its initial intake.

pairs.question: What happened to the Civil Affairs Police Force (CAPF) on 1 April 1946, and why?
pairs.answer: On 1 April 1946, the Civil Affairs Police Force (CAPF) was renamed to the Malayan Union Police Force. This change was made in line with the establishment of the Malayan Union, reflecting the reorganization of British Malaya's administrative structure at that time.
